title: An investigation to offer conclusive recommendations on suitable benefit/cost criteria-based normalization methods for TOPSIS
author: Krishnan, Hamid, et al.
description: Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) is a popular multi-criteria decision-making method that ranks the available alternatives by examining the ideal-positive and ideal-negative solutions for each decision criterion. The first step of using TOPSIS is to normalize the presence of incommensurable data in the decision matrix. There are several normalization methods, and the choice of these methods does affect TOPSIS results. As such, some efforts were made in the past to compare and recommend suitable normalization methods for TOPSIS. However, such studies merely compared a limited collection of normalization methods or used a noncomprehensive procedure to evaluate each method's suitability, leading to equivocal recommendations. This study, therefore, employed an alternate, comprehensive procedure to evaluate and recommend suitable benefit/cost criteria-based normalization methods for TOPSIS (out of ten methods extracted from past literature). The procedure was devised based on three evaluation metrics: the average Spearman's rank correlation, average Pearson correlation, and standard deviation metrics, combined with the Borda count technique.‚Ä¢The first study examined the suitability of ten benefit/cost criteria-based normalization methods over TOPSIS.‚Ä¢Users should combine the sum-based method and vector method into the TOPSIS application for safer decision-making.‚Ä¢The maximum method (version I) or J√ºttler's-K√∂rth's method has an identical effect on TOPSIS results.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: We developed a real decision matrix involving 30 alternatives, i.e. smartphone models in the current marketplace, to serve as the primary input for our comparative study (see Table¬Ý3). The decision matrix compiles the data of 30 alternatives, A1,A2 ,‚Ä¶,A30 based on five smartphone criteria suggested by [56]. Those criteria are the price measured in Malaysian Ringgit (C1), the screen size measured in inches (C2), the pixel density measured in pixels per centimetre (C3), the thickness measured in millimetres (C4), and the mass measured in grams (C5). The data were gleaned from various popular gadget websites, e.g. www.stuff.tv, https://www.techadvisor.com, and https://technave.com/gadget. In other words, the size of the decision matrix is sufficiently large to yield reliable results, and it has a mixture of both the benefit and cost criteria measured with different units, making it suitable for this study. Note that C1, C4, and C5 are cost criteria, and the remaining are benefit criteria.Table 3Decision matrix of 30 smartphone models.Table 3ModelPrice in Malaysia Ringgit (C1)Screen size in inches (C2)Pixel density in pixels per centimetre (C3)Thickness in millimetres (C4)Mass in grams (C5)A147096.82008.9234A249996.1487.85206A313286.7508.9212A432996.7508.5205A536326.7508.5218A618706.74508.9199A731995.4127.65141A819696.1128.9178A951996.5128.2185A1030896.7507.3183A1147596.2126.3263A1216995.9509.1169A1326996.785010.3239A1459997.9546.1261A1542996.81508.8219A1634996.73547.8191A1738636.85016191A1834996.73508.4210A1932206.36508185A2030996.8508.9228A2133596.78128.5196A2242726.7850.39.3214.9A2340996.74508.5205A2479997.85011.1255A2526996.671089.5220A2633996.81508.4234A2729996.92649.9259A2835896.81088.9228A2914996.672008.9208.4A3043196.78509.1215Source: www.stuff.tv, https://www.techadvisor.com, and https://technave.com/gadget.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: All in all, the study's investigation based on the developed decision matrix can be summarized into three major stages. The stages and steps involved in each stage are outlined in the following sub-sections.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Stage 1 - Application of TOPSIS with the combination of different normalization methods

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Stage 1 aimed at determining the scores and ranks of the alternatives available in the decision matrix by employing TOPSIS with the combination of ten normalization methods listed in Table¬Ý1. Following are the exact steps involved in Stage 1:(a)Step 1- We first normalized the data in the decision matrix using the N1 method to convert them into a common scale using (2). At the end of Step 1, a normalized matrix as expressed by (12) was derived.(12)[rij]=[r11r12‚ãØr1nr21r22‚ãØr2n‚ãÆ‚ãÆ‚ãÆ‚ãÆrm1rm2‚ãØrmn]where rij is the normalized data of alternative i over criterion j, where i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m and j=1,2,‚Ä¶,n.(b)Step 2 - We assigned the weight for each criterion, wj and developed the weighted normalized matrix, [Vij] as expressed in (13). Note that for this study, we used the criteria weights estimated by Krishnan et¬Ýal. [56], where these weights were estimated via the combination of various objective weighting techniques. Table¬Ý4 shows the weight of each criterion.(13)[Vij]=[V11V12‚ãØV1nV21V22‚ãØV2n‚ãÆ‚ãÆ‚ãÆ‚ãÆVm1Vm2‚ãØVmn],Table 4The weight of each criterion.Table 4CriterionC1C2C3C4C5Weight0.20210.19560.20310.18740.2118Source: [56].where Vij=wj√órij.(c)Step 3 - We determined the positive-ideal, A* and negative-ideal, A‚àí solutions based on the weighted normalized matrix, using Eqs.¬Ý(14) and (15).(14)A*={V1*,V2*,‚Ä¶,Vn*}(15)A‚àí={V1‚àí,V2‚àí,‚Ä¶,Vn‚àí}where Vj*=maxVij; Vj‚àí=minVij;i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m; j=1,2,‚Ä¶,n.(d)Step 4 - We calculated the separation measure for alternatives using n-dimensional Euclidean distance. The separation/distance of each alternative from the positive-ideal solution, Si*, can be obtained using Eq.¬Ý(16). Similarly, the separation of each alternative from the negative-ideal solution, Si‚àí can be computed based on Eq.¬Ý(17).(16)Si*=‚àëj=1n(Vij‚àíVj*)2;i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m(17)Si‚àí=‚àëj=1n(Vij‚àíVj‚àí)2;i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m(e)Step 5 - We determined the final alternative score for each alternative, Pi, using Eq.¬Ý(18), with i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m. The alternatives were then ranked based on Pi, where higher Pi indicates better preference over alternative i.(18)Pi=Si‚àíSi*+Si‚àí;i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m(f)Step 6 - Steps 1‚Äì5 were repeated based on the other nine normalization methods by properly applying equations (3) - (11). Note that we performed all the calculations with the aid of Microsoft Excel.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Stage 2 - Evaluation of normalization methods based on three metrics

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: In Stage 2, the results obtained from all the different normalization methods were further evaluated based on the three metrics, namely, average Spearman's rank correlation metric, average Pearson correlation metric, and standard deviation metric, to ensure a comprehensive suitability check. Each metric's role and computational steps are available in the following explanation.(a)Step 1 - The alternative ranks resulting from all ten methods were analyzed using Spearman's rank correlation test. The test is notably applied to determine the degree of consistency between two different sets of ranks [57]. In other words, this test was carried out to understand each method's ability to provide reliable alternative ranks. The Spearman's correlation coefficient between any two ranks was computed using Eq.¬Ý(19) [58,59].(19)ks=1‚àí6‚àëdi2m(m2‚àí1),where ks= Spearman's rank correlation coefficient between the alternative ranks resulting from normalization methods, x and y; di= rank difference for an alternative i based on the normalization methods, x and y; m= total alternatives; i=1,2,‚Ä¶,m. The average ks corresponding to each normalization was then computed, where higher ks implies the method's better ability to produce reliable alternative ranks [60].¬Ý(b)Step 2 - The Pearson correlation coefficient is a statistical measure that is widely used to study the degree of consistency between two interval or ratio-based variables [61]. As such, this study performed the Pearson correlation test to measure each normalization method's ability to generate reliable alternative scores. The test indeed has been employed by various scholars for a similar purpose. For example, Kabassi et¬Ýal. [62] analyzed the Pearson correlations between the scores of four museum websites estimated by different MCDM methods, including fuzzy VIKOR and TOPSIS, in search of the most reliable method.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction:
The Pearson correlation coefficient between every two sets of alternative scores was calculated using Eq.¬Ý(20)
[63].(20)rs=‚àë(Pix‚àíP¬Øx)(Piy‚àíP¬Øy)‚àë(Pix‚àíP¬Øx)2‚àë(Piy‚àíP¬Øy)2where rs= Pearson correlation coefficient of alternative scores identified using normalization method, x and y; Pix¬Ý=¬Ýscore of an alternative i identified using the normalization method, x; Piy¬Ý=¬Ýscore of an alternative i identified using the normalization method, y; Px¬Ø¬Ý=¬Ýaverage alternative score of normalization method, k; Py¬Ø¬Ý=¬Ýaverage alternative score of normalization method, y; i=1,2,..,m.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Note that a rs value ranges between ‚àí1 to 1. A value closer to 1 indicates higher consistency/similarity, and vice versa if it is closer to ‚àí1 [64]. The calculated Rs values were then averaged out according to each normalization method, where higher rs implies the method's better ability to estimate reliable alternative scores [65].(c)Step 3 - The standard deviation of the alternative scores resulting from each normalization method was computed using Eq.¬Ý(21), where a higher standard deviation indicates the method's better ability to distribute distinctive scores across the least to most preferred alternatives.(21)SDx=‚àëi=1m(Pix‚àíP¬Øx)2m‚àí1where SDx= standard deviation of a normalization method, x; Pix¬Ý=¬Ýscore of an alternative, i by a normalization method, x; Px¬Ø¬Ý=¬Ýaverage alternative scores of normalization method, x; m¬Ý=¬Ýtotal alternatives; i=1,2,..,m.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Stage 3- Finalization of suitable normalization methods

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Since the normalization methods showcased different performance ranks across all three evaluation metrics, it was difficult to conclude which methods suit TOPSIS. Thus, the analysis was furthered by aggregating these ranks by applying the Borda technique. Although some earlier studies used the plurality technique to aggregate such ranks, this study decided to opt for the Borda technique. The decision was made in light of the technique's ability to control information loss by mathematically capturing the entire relative ranks across different evaluation metrics [66,67]. Also, previous findings hint that the technique can be wise enough to ensure only the normalization method that has acceptable decent performance across all the metrics is chosen to be the best [68].

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Following were the steps adhered to determine the final suitability rank of each normalization method using the Borda technique [69,70]:(a)Step 1- Points were assigned to the normalization methods according to their rank over the first metric, i.e., the average Spearman's rank correlation metric. The method ranked first was assigned with (totalranks‚àí1) points, (totalranks‚àí2) points for the method ranked second, and so.(b)Step 2- Step 1 was repeated based on the other two metrics, i.e. the average Pearson correlation metric and standard deviation metric.(c)Step 3- The aggregated Borda score of each normalization was then obtained by summing up the points.(d)Step 4: The normalization methods were re-ranked ascendingly based on the aggregated Borda scores, where each score implies the method's overall suitability over TOPSIS. A higher Borda score, of course, indicates that the method is more suitable for TOPSIS.

steps_raw.title: Methodology
steps_raw.instruction: The flowchart and pseudocode illustrating the inputs, steps and outputs of all three stages are presented via Fig.¬Ý1 and Table¬Ý5 to allow easy understanding and quick conversion into a proper programming language for future use.Fig. 1flowchart for Stages 1, 2, and 3.Fig 1Table 5Pseudocode for Stages 1, 2 and 3.Table 5Algorithm 1 Pseudocode for Stage 1- Application of TOPSIS with the combination of different normalization methods1: matrix ‚Üê generate(alt, crit) //Generate decision matrix2: N ‚Üê 10 // N is normalization methods3:fori¬Ý=¬Ý1 to Ndo//Step 1: Normalize decision matrix4:norm_score ‚Üê norm_matrix(matrix) //Step 2: Derive weighted normalized matrix5: weight_score ‚Üê weight_matrix(norm_score, crit_weight)//Step 3: Determine positive / negative ideal solutions6: for num_of_weight_score[n] do7: positive ‚Üê max(weight_score)8: negative ‚Üê min(weight score)//Step 4: Calculate separation of positive/negative solutions9: calculate_separation(positive, negative)//Step 5: Determine alternative scores and ranks10: for num_of_alt[n] do11: alt_score ‚Üê (positive/sum(positive+negative))12: alt_rank(alt_score)13: end for14: end for//Step 6: Repeat Step 1‚Äì5 for other 9 methods in loop15: end forAlgorithm 2 Pseudocode for Stage 2-Evaluation of normalization methods based on three metrics1: N ‚Üê 10 // N is normalization methods2: fori¬Ý=¬Ý1 to Ndo//Step 1: Spearman's rank correlation test3: spm_rank ‚Üê avg[calculate_correlations(alt_rank=i, alt_rank=i)]//Step 2: Pearson correlation coefficient4: pearson_rank ‚Üê avg[calculate_correlations(alt_rank=i,alt_rank=i)]//Step 3: Standard deviation of alternative scores5: stdv_rank ‚Üê calculate_stdv(alt_score)6: end forAlgorithm 3 Pseudocode for Stage 3- Finalization of suitable normalization methods1: N ‚Üê 10 // N is normalization methods2: fori¬Ý=¬Ý1 to Ndo3: total_rank ‚Üê count(alt_rank)//Step 1: Assign points to N based on their rank Spearman's rank4: spm_point ‚Üê assign_point(total_rank - spm_rank)//Step 2: Assign points to N based on Pearson rank5: pearson_point ‚Üê assign_point(total_rank - pearson_rank)//Step 3: Assign points to N based on standard deviation rank6: stdv_point ‚Üê assign_point(total_rank - stdv_rank)//Step 4: Aggregate Borda points7: borda_score ‚Üê sum(pearson_point, spm_point, stdv_point)//Step 5: Re-ranking based on Borda scores8: ascend_rank(borda_score)9: end for
